subtopic_detail: Empirical therapy for adult bacterial meningitis with Listeria cover
stem: You are the medical registrar in the emergency department reviewing a 65-year-old man with 12 hours of fever, severe headache, photophobia and neck stiffness. GCS is 13. No focal neurology. No skin rash. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. He takes no immunosuppressants. Lumbar puncture has been performed and the CSF analysis is back.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.5 °C, HR 116, BP 132/82, RR 22, SpO2 96% room air, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Marked neck stiffness; positive Kernig sign; no focal deficit; no skin rash; clear chest
data_table.Investigations: CSF: WCC 1200/microL (90 percent neutrophils), protein 3.2 g/L, glucose 1.2 mmol/L (paired BSL 6.4 mmol/L), Gram stain pending; FBC and U&E unremarkable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly as the sole antibiotic until cultures return
options.rationale: In patients over 50 or immunocompromised, Listeria cover with benzylpenicillin is required alongside ceftriaxone. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous amoxicillin and gentamicin only awaiting culture and sensitivity results
options.rationale: Amoxicillin and gentamicin under-treat the typical pneumococcal and meningococcal organisms; cephalosporin cover is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Ceftriaxone with benzylpenicillin Listeria cover and dexamethasone at standard adult doses
options.rationale: Adult patients over 50 or immunocompromised need ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly for pneumococcus and meningococcus, benzylpenicillin 2.4 g 4-hourly for Listeria, and dexamethasone 10 mg 6-hourly reduces neurological sequelae in pneumococcal disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin alone with a target trough of 15-20 mg/L pending sensitivities
options.rationale: Vancomycin alone does not cover Listeria and is reserved for adjunctive cover of penicillin-resistant pneumococci. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Defer all antibiotics until Gram stain and culture results are available later today
options.rationale: Delaying therapy in bacterial meningitis worsens mortality and neurological outcome; empirical therapy precedes Gram stain results. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Empirical therapy for adult bacterial meningitis in Australia is ceftriaxone for pneumococcal and meningococcal cover, with the addition of benzylpenicillin for Listeria in patients over 50, immunocompromised or pregnant. Adjunctive dexamethasone 10 mg every 6 hours is given with or before the first antibiotic dose for suspected pneumococcal disease to reduce neurological sequelae. Lumbar puncture, blood cultures and CT brain in selected cases guide ongoing therapy.
key_points: Ceftriaxone covers pneumococcus and meningococcus.
Add benzylpenicillin in patients over 50 or immunocompromised for Listeria.
Dexamethasone 10 mg 6-hourly reduces sequelae in pneumococcal disease.
Do not delay antibiotics for imaging or Gram stain.
explanation.pearls: Indications to do CT before lumbar puncture include focal neurology, GCS under 10, recent seizure or significant immunosuppression; if any are present, draw blood cultures, give empirical antibiotics and dexamethasone immediately, then arrange CT, and proceed to lumbar puncture once safe.

subtopic_detail: Acute management of severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes
stem: You are the medical registrar called to the emergency department to assess a 68-year-old man with chronic kidney disease stage 3 on perindopril. Routine bloods done by the GP this morning returned a potassium of 6.7 mmol/L. An ECG taken on arrival shows peaked T waves throughout the precordial leads, reduced P wave amplitude and a QRS duration of 110 ms. He is alert and asymptomatic.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 152/86, HR 64 regular, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Alert and oriented; chest clear; no focal neurology; no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Potassium 6.7 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, urea 14 mmol/L, creatinine 220 micromol/L (baseline 150), bicarbonate 18 mmol/L; ECG: peaked T waves, low P, QRS 110 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL over 2-5 minutes with continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes (peaked T, P attenuation, QRS widening) requires immediate cardiac membrane stabilisation; calcium gluconate works in minutes and is the first step. (Source: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury Guidelines)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 20 mg alone as the sole urgent intervention to lower potassium now
options.rationale: Salbutamol shifts potassium intracellularly but does not stabilise the myocardium; it follows calcium when ECG changes are present. (Source: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury Guidelines)

options.text: Oral cation exchange resin alone with planned outpatient repeat in 24-48 hours' time
options.rationale: Cation exchange resins remove potassium slowly and are inadequate when ECG changes are present and the K is 6.7. (Source: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous insulin and dextrose alone without prior calcium for myocardial stabilisation
options.rationale: Insulin and dextrose shift potassium effectively but do not stabilise the myocardium; calcium should precede them when ECG changes are evident. (Source: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury Guidelines)

options.text: Cease perindopril alone with a planned repeat potassium check in 48 hours' time
options.rationale: Stopping the ACE inhibitor addresses the cause but does not treat the immediate cardiac risk from severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes. (Source: KDIGO Acute Kidney Injury Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes is a medical emergency. Management proceeds in three steps: stabilise the myocardium with intravenous calcium gluconate within minutes; shift potassium intracellularly using insulin with dextrose and nebulised salbutamol; and remove potassium from the body using diuresis, cation exchange resin or dialysis. Reversible causes including ACE inhibitor or potassium-sparing diuretic therapy should be addressed and follow-up potassium checks scheduled.
key_points: Calcium gluconate stabilises the myocardium within minutes.
Insulin-dextrose and salbutamol shift potassium intracellularly.
Resin or dialysis removes potassium from the body.
Cease ACE inhibitor and review longer-term causes.
explanation.pearls: Calcium gluconate has a shorter duration than the potassium shift you generate, so repeat ECGs after 30-60 minutes; an evolving QRS or sine wave despite shifts means it is time for dialysis, not another insulin bolus.

subtopic_detail: Acute HIV seroconversion diagnosis with negative antibody screen
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 28-year-old man who has sex with men with a 10-day history of fever, sore throat, generalised lymphadenopathy and a maculopapular rash on the trunk. He reports unprotected receptive anal intercourse with a new partner 3 weeks ago. He has had no recent travel. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. Fourth-generation HIV combined antigen and antibody assay is negative. Streptococcal throat swab, EBV serology and CMV serology are all negative.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 °C, HR 96, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Maculopapular rash on trunk; cervical, axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy; mild pharyngeal erythema; no oral ulceration; no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: HIV combined Ag/Ab fourth generation: negative; EBV IgM negative; CMV IgM negative; throat swab negative for strep; FBC: lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Repeat the HIV combined Ag/Ab test in 12 weeks for a definitive negative result
options.rationale: Repeat antibody testing alone delays the diagnosis during the window period when transmission risk to partners is high. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Order HIV RNA viral load PCR now to detect early infection within the window period
options.rationale: Acute HIV viral load PCR detects infection from around 10 days post-exposure and is the appropriate next test when fourth-generation Ag/Ab is negative but clinical suspicion is high. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Send CD4 count alone to assess immune status before deciding on next investigations
options.rationale: CD4 count assesses prognosis in established infection rather than diagnosing acute seroconversion; it is not useful in the window period. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Commence empirical antiretrovirals immediately without diagnostic confirmation now
options.rationale: Antiretrovirals require a confirmed diagnosis and specialist consultation; empirical commencement is not appropriate primary care practice. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Discharge with reassurance and review in 4 weeks if symptoms have not resolved
options.rationale: Symptoms strongly suggest acute seroconversion; reassurance and delayed review risks ongoing transmission and disease progression. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion presents with non-specific viral symptoms including fever, lymphadenopathy, pharyngitis and maculopapular rash within 2 to 4 weeks of exposure. The fourth-generation combined antigen and antibody test detects infection from about 2 weeks. When the fourth-generation test is negative but clinical suspicion is high during the window period, HIV RNA viral load PCR detects infection from about 10 days. Early diagnosis allows partner notification, prevention of transmission and prompt initiation of treatment.
key_points: Fourth-generation Ag/Ab detects from 2 weeks post-exposure.
HIV RNA PCR detects from 10 days; useful in window period.
Treat partners and notify with patient agreement.
Refer for specialist initiation of antiretrovirals.
explanation.pearls: In the same consultation, take the opportunity to discuss post-exposure prophylaxis for any other recent unprotected encounters and pre-exposure prophylaxis once acute infection is excluded; many MSM at risk are not yet aware of PrEP availability through Medicare in Australia.

subtopic_detail: Necrotising soft tissue infection time-critical surgical referral
stem: You are the medical registrar in the emergency department reviewing a 55-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes who presents with 12 hours of rapidly progressive, severely painful swelling of the left lower leg following trivial garden trauma. He looks toxic with rigors. The leg has dusky erythema with two haemorrhagic bullae and a small area of crepitus over the medial calf. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. He has no documented antibiotic allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2 °C, HR 124, BP 96/58, RR 24, SpO2 95% room air
data_table.Examination: Dusky erythema with two haemorrhagic bullae; crepitus over medial calf; pain out of proportion; brisk capillary refill becomes sluggish at edges
data_table.Investigations: CRP 380 mg/L, WCC 24 x10^9/L, Hb 110 g/L, sodium 128 mmol/L, creatinine 180 micromol/L, glucose 18 mmol/L, lactate 3.8 mmol/L; LRINEC score 11

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Outpatient oral cephalexin 500 mg four times daily with review in primary care in 48 hours
options.rationale: Oral therapy and outpatient review are dangerously inadequate; the clinical picture is necrotising soft tissue infection with high mortality. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous cefazolin 2 g 8-hourly as monotherapy without referral for surgical exploration
options.rationale: Antibiotic monotherapy without surgical debridement does not control source in necrotising fasciitis; mortality climbs steeply for every hour of delay to surgery. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Arrange punch skin biopsy first to confirm the diagnosis prior to any surgical consultation
options.rationale: Skin biopsy delays definitive source control; diagnosis of necrotising fasciitis is clinical and surgical, supported by laboratory clues such as LRINEC. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the affected leg before any surgical exploration takes place
options.rationale: MRI delays surgery and adds no value when clinical and laboratory features already strongly indicate necrotising infection; cross-sectional imaging is selectively used. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Urgent surgical exploration with debridement plus IV piperacillin-tazobactam, clindamycin and vancomycin
options.rationale: Time-critical surgical debridement plus broad empirical antibiotic cover (piperacillin-tazobactam for Gram-negatives and anaerobes, clindamycin for toxin suppression, vancomycin for MRSA) is the Australian standard for necrotising soft tissue infection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising soft tissue infection is a surgical emergency. Pain out of proportion, rapid progression, systemic toxicity, haemorrhagic bullae, crepitus and dusky erythema are key clinical clues. The LRINEC score (CRP, WCC, Hb, sodium, creatinine, glucose) supports clinical suspicion but should never delay surgery. Empirical antibiotics combine broad Gram-negative and anaerobic cover with toxin suppression by clindamycin and MRSA cover by vancomycin. Survival is inversely related to time from presentation to operating theatre.
key_points: Pain out of proportion and crepitus are key clinical clues.
LRINEC supports but does not replace clinical suspicion.
Surgical debridement is time-critical for survival.
Empirical cover: pip-taz plus clindamycin plus vancomycin.
explanation.pearls: Group A streptococcal necrotising fasciitis often presents with hyperacute onset and disproportionate pain before visible skin changes; if the pain story is dramatic and the leg looks deceptively mild, lean towards calling the surgeons early rather than waiting for the rash to declare itself.

sources.label: IDSA Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

subtopic_detail: Pharmacotherapy initiation in older-onset Parkinson disease
stem: You are the neurology registrar reviewing a 68-year-old retired teacher with an 18-month history of right-hand resting tremor, bradykinesia, mild rigidity, mild gait shuffle and difficulty with handwriting that is interfering with daily activities. There are no falls, no orthostasis, no early visual hallucinations and no cognitive impairment on MoCA (28/30). He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Examination: Right-hand resting tremor 4 Hz; cogwheel rigidity at wrist; mild hypomimia; bradykinesia on finger taps; mild shuffling gait; no postural instability; MoCA 28/30
data_table.Investigations: TFTs, B12 and folate normal; FBC and U&E unremarkable; DAT-SPECT abnormal asymmetric nigrostriatal uptake

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Rasagiline 1 mg daily as monotherapy aiming to defer levodopa for at least 12 months
options.rationale: MAO-B inhibitors give only modest symptomatic benefit and are inadequate when handwriting and daily activities are clearly affected in older patients. (Source: MOSAIC Parkinson Guidelines)

options.text: Pramipexole 0.125 mg three times daily titrating to symptom control with no levodopa
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists in patients over 65 risk hallucinations, daytime sleepiness, impulse control disorders and oedema and are not preferred first-line at this age. (Source: MOSAIC Parkinson Guidelines)

options.text: Amantadine 100 mg twice daily alone for symptom control over the next 6-12 months
options.rationale: Amantadine offers only modest benefit and is not standard first-line monotherapy in early Parkinson disease with functional impairment. (Source: MOSAIC Parkinson Guidelines)

options.text: Levodopa with peripheral decarboxylase inhibitor titrated from low dose to symptom control
options.rationale: Levodopa with carbidopa or benserazide is first-line in patients over 65 with functional impairment; it produces the largest symptomatic benefit with lower hallucination risk than dopamine agonists in this age group. (Source: MOSAIC Parkinson Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for deep brain stimulation now before any oral pharmacotherapy is initiated
options.rationale: Deep brain stimulation is reserved for selected patients with established motor fluctuations or refractory tremor after years of pharmacotherapy; it is not first-line treatment. (Source: MOSAIC Parkinson Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Parkinson disease pharmacotherapy choice balances symptomatic benefit against motor and non-motor complications. In patients over 65 with clear functional impairment, levodopa with a peripheral decarboxylase inhibitor is first-line because it produces the largest symptomatic benefit with lower risk of hallucinations and impulse control disorders than dopamine agonists. Younger patients with mild disease may start a dopamine agonist or MAO-B inhibitor to defer motor fluctuations and dyskinesia. Multidisciplinary care and falls prevention complement pharmacotherapy.
key_points: Levodopa is preferred first-line in patients over 65.
Dopamine agonists risk hallucinations and impulse problems in older patients.
Younger patients may use agonists or MAO-B inhibitors to delay levodopa.
Multidisciplinary care supports long-term function.
explanation.pearls: Sudden withdrawal of dopaminergic therapy can precipitate a parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia syndrome that resembles neuroleptic malignant syndrome; always continue or replace dopaminergic therapy during admissions, even when patients cannot swallow, using rotigotine patch or apomorphine if needed.
